question: Why would a team choose Kotlin over Java for a new backend microservice? What trade-offs would you consider?
keyPoints: Null safety eliminates NPE class of bugs at compile time
30-50% less boilerplate (data classes, default params, type inference)
Coroutines for efficient async without callback complexity
Full Java interop — gradual migration, same libraries
Trade-offs: slower compilation, steeper learning curve, kapt complexity
modelAnswer: Kotlin reduces production defects through compile-time null safety and encourages immutable designs through val-by-default and data classes. For a new service, the 30-50% boilerplate reduction improves maintainability. Coroutines provide efficient concurrency without reactive complexity. The main trade-offs are slower compilation, a learning curve for the team, and some framework friction with annotation processing. I would choose Kotlin for a new service but would not rewrite existing Java services without business justification.

followUps.question: How would you handle a team where half the developers don't know Kotlin?
followUps.answerHint: Gradual adoption, pair programming, Java-first modules that Kotlin consumes

followUps.question: What problems have you seen when mixing Kotlin and Java in the same project?
followUps.answerHint: Platform types, null annotations needed on Java side, build complexity

question: How does Kotlin compile to the JVM? What does this mean for performance and interoperability?
keyPoints: Compiles to standard JVM bytecode (same as Java)
No custom runtime required
Properties become getters/setters, data classes generate equals/hashCode/copy
Suspend functions become state machines
Performance is equivalent — differences come from allocations, not the language
modelAnswer: Kotlin compiles to standard JVM bytecode executable by any JVM. There is no separate runtime. Properties become getter/setter pairs, data classes generate standard object methods, and suspend functions compile to state machines with continuations. Performance is essentially equivalent to Java — any differences come from specific patterns like lambda allocations, which Kotlin mitigates through inline functions.

question: What does this code print?
code:
val items = mutableListOf(1, 2, 3)
val copy = items
copy.add(4)
println(items.size)
println(items === copy)
explanation: val makes the reference immutable, not the object. 'copy' and 'items' point to the same mutable list. Adding to 'copy' modifies the same list. === checks reference equality, which is true.

question: Explain the difference between val, const val, and var. When would you use each?
keyPoints: var: mutable reference, reassignable
val: immutable reference (like final), assigned once at runtime
const val: compile-time constant, must be primitive or String, top-level or companion
val does NOT mean the object is immutable — just the reference
modelAnswer: var is a mutable reference — use it for loop counters or accumulating state where necessary. val is an immutable reference assigned once, possibly at runtime — use it for everything else (95% of variables). const val is a compile-time constant inlined by the compiler — use it for truly fixed values like config keys, header names, or error codes. Important: val prevents reassignment but does not make the object immutable — a val pointing to a MutableList can still have elements added.

question: How does Kotlin's null safety work? How do you handle nullable types coming from Java code?
keyPoints: Types are non-nullable by default (String vs String?)
Compiler enforces null handling at every usage
Safe call (?.), Elvis (?:), smart casts
Java returns platform types (String!) — nullability unknown
Add @Nullable/@NonNull to Java APIs, or declare explicit type on Kotlin side
modelAnswer: Kotlin splits every type into nullable (String?) and non-nullable (String). The compiler refuses to call methods on nullable types without handling null first — via safe calls, Elvis operator, or smart casts after null checks. When consuming Java APIs, Kotlin sees 'platform types' where nullability is unknown. The safe approach is to explicitly declare the expected type on the Kotlin side, or annotate the Java code with @Nullable/@NonNull. I avoid !! in production code — it defeats the purpose of null safety.

question: What does this code print?
code:
val name: String? = null
val length = name?.length ?: -1
val upper = name?.uppercase() ?: "UNKNOWN"
println("$length $upper")
explanation: name is null. name?.length returns null, Elvis (?:) provides -1. name?.uppercase() returns null, Elvis provides "UNKNOWN".

question: When would you use a sealed class instead of a nullable return type?
keyPoints: Nullable only communicates present/absent
Sealed class communicates WHY something is absent
Compiler enforces exhaustive when-checks
Better for operations with multiple failure modes
modelAnswer: A nullable return type communicates only two states: present or absent. A sealed class can represent multiple outcomes with context — Found, NotFound, Error, Unauthorized, etc. The compiler enforces that callers handle all cases. I use nullable for simple lookups where absence is obvious, and sealed classes when the caller needs to distinguish between different reasons for absence or failure.

followUps.question: How does this compare to Java's Optional?
followUps.answerHint: Optional is heap-allocated, limited to present/absent. Sealed class is richer and zero-allocation (value classes).

question: How do extension functions work internally? What are their limitations?
keyPoints: Compiled to static methods with receiver as first parameter
Resolved statically (no virtual dispatch/polymorphism)
Cannot access private members
Cannot override existing class members
Syntactic sugar — no runtime cost
modelAnswer: Extension functions compile to static methods where the receiver becomes the first parameter. This means they are resolved at compile time based on the declared type, not the runtime type — no polymorphism. They cannot access private members of the class and cannot override existing methods. Their power is in discoverability and readability — you write list.secondOrNull() instead of ListUtils.secondOrNull(list). But understanding the static nature is critical to avoid surprises with inheritance.

question: What does this code print? Why?
explanation: Extension functions are resolved statically based on the declared type of the variable, not the runtime type. The parameter is declared as Shape, so Shape.name() is called even though the actual object is a Circle. This is because extensions compile to static methods — there is no virtual dispatch.

question: What is the difference between eager collection operations and sequences in Kotlin? When would you use each?
keyPoints: Eager: creates intermediate collections at each step, processes all elements
Sequence: lazy, element-by-element, no intermediate collections
Sequences benefit large collections, multiple steps, early termination
Eager is fine for small collections or single operations
Inline functions make eager lambdas allocation-free
modelAnswer: Eager operations (filter, map on List) create a new collection at each step and process all elements. Sequences are lazy — they process one element through the entire chain before moving to the next, creating no intermediate collections. Use sequences for large collections with multiple transformations or early termination (first, take). For small collections, eager is simpler and equally fast because Kotlin's inline functions eliminate lambda allocation overhead.

question: Explain inline functions. Why does Kotlin use them extensively in the standard library?
keyPoints: Compiler copies function body at call site (no function object created)
Eliminates lambda allocation overhead
Standard library (let, apply, filter, map) all inline
Enables non-local returns
Trade-off: increases bytecode size
modelAnswer: Inline functions have their body copied at the call site by the compiler. This eliminates the overhead of creating Function objects for lambdas. The standard library extensively uses inline for scope functions (let, apply, run) and collection operations (filter, map). This means using lambdas in Kotlin has zero allocation cost — unlike Java where every lambda creates a synthetic class. The trade-off is increased bytecode size, so only small utility functions should be inline.
